criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the three core components of AutoSteer: SAS-based layer selection, safety prober, and refusal head.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the Safety Awareness Score (SAS) method, including contrastive vector computation and pairwise cosine similarity aggregation.
The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to understand the safety prober architecture: an MLP with a single hidden layer of 64 dimensions, ReLU activation, and sigmoid output.
The agent has read Section 3.4 completely to understand the adaptive steering mechanism: the thresholding function α(s), steered embedding computation, and modified logit derivation.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the datasets (VLSafe, ToViLaG+, MMMU, RealWorldQA) and the baseline setup.
The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to check the implementation details: Layer 20 selected for LLaVA-OV, Layer 24 for Chameleon, threshold τ = 0.5, and steering intensity ε = 0.1.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the main results on Attack Success Rate (ASR) and general accuracy across all benchmarks and models.
The agent has read Appendix A to check the exact prompts used for safe counterpart data construction (A.1) and ToViLaG+ augmentation (A.2, A.3, A.4).
The agent has read Appendix B to understand the evaluation protocols: direct answer comparison for LLaVA-OV general accuracy, regex matching for Chameleon, and GPT-4o binary classification for ASR.
The plan includes loading and sampling VLSafe (3,000 entries for refusal head and prober training, 500 entries for VLSafe Examine evaluation).
The plan includes constructing safe counterpart data from VLSafe toxic queries using GPT-4o with the prompt specified in Appendix A.1.
The plan includes the three-stage AutoSteer training pipeline: positive/negative pair creation, SAS layer selection with prober training, and refusal head training.
The plan includes computing SAS for each layer using N = 3,000 contrastive pairs derived from both VLSafe and constructed toxic-safe pairs.
The plan includes training the safety prober (MLP) on layer activations from the SAS-selected layer using 3,000 toxic and 3,000 safe examples.
The plan includes training the Refusal Head using the LM-Steer framework on 3,000 VLSafe alignment entries, with the refusal target being 'I am sorry, but I can't assist with that'.
The plan includes augmenting the LLaVA-OV Refusal Head training with 3,000 toxic images from ToViLaG (no overlap with test images).
The plan includes loading and sampling ToViLaG+ with three test types (text-only, image-only, text+image toxicity), 500 entries each.
The plan includes loading and sampling MMMU (500 entries) and RealWorldQA (500 entries) for general capability evaluation.
The plan includes the Steer baseline implementation using LM-Steer with global (non-conditional) steering applied to all inputs.
The plan includes implementing ASR evaluation using GPT-4o as a binary toxicity classifier with the prompt from Appendix B.2.
The plan includes the ε sensitivity analysis varying steering intensity from 0 to beyond 0.2 on VLSafe for LLaVA-OV and Chameleon.
The plan includes the SAS score comparison across layers for both models and prober accuracy evaluation across training epochs.
The code for loading and sampling VLSafe (3,000 training entries and 500 evaluation entries) has been implemented.
The code for constructing safe counterparts from VLSafe using GPT-4o with the exact prompt from Appendix A.1 has been implemented.
The code for loading and sampling the ToViLaG+ dataset with three test subsets (text-only, image-only, text+image, 500 entries each) has been implemented.
The code for constructing ToViLaG+ text+image toxicity data using Grok-2 with the prompt from Appendix A.2 has been implemented.
The code for constructing ToViLaG+ text-only toxicity data using Grok-2 with the prompt from Appendix A.3 has been implemented.
The code for constructing ToViLaG+ image-only toxicity data using GPT-4o with the prompt from Appendix A.4 has been implemented.
The code for loading and sampling MMMU (500 entries) and RealWorldQA (500 entries) for general capability evaluation has been implemented.
The code for loading the LLaVA-OV-7B model and extracting intermediate layer activations has been implemented.
The code for loading the Chameleon-7B model and extracting intermediate layer activations has been implemented.
The code for extracting hidden state activation vectors h_l(x) at each intermediate layer l for a given input x has been implemented.
The code for computing contrastive vectors δ_l = h_l(x_toxic) - h_l(x_safe) as defined in Equation 7 has been implemented.
The code for computing the Safety Awareness Score (SAS) at each layer as the mean pairwise cosine similarity over N sampled contrastive vectors as defined in Equation 8 has been implemented.
The code for automatically selecting the optimal probing layer L as the layer with the highest SAS score has been implemented.
The safety prober P has been implemented as an MLP with a single hidden layer of 64 dimensions and ReLU activation, as defined in Equation 10.
The safety prober weight dimensions are correctly implemented: W1 ∈ R^(64×d), W2 ∈ R^(1×64), with bias vectors b1 ∈ R^64 and b2 ∈ R, and sigmoid output.
The safety prober output s = P(h_L(x)) ∈ [0,1] representing toxicity probability as defined in Equation 9 has been implemented.
The safety prober training code uses activations extracted from the SAS-selected layer L, trained on 3,000 toxic and 3,000 safe examples.
The threshold function α(s) has been implemented as defined in Equation 11: α(s) = 0 if s < τ, α(s) = 1 if s ≥ τ, with τ = 0.5.
The steered embedding computation e'_v ← (I + εα(s)·W)e_v has been implemented as defined in Equation 12.
The modified logit computation z̃_t = H_t · E' = H_t · (E + εα(s) · (E·W)^T) has been implemented as defined in Equation 13.
The Refusal Head matrix W has been trained on 3,000 toxic samples from VLSafe using the LM-Steer framework, with the refusal target 'I am sorry, but I can't assist with that'.
The LLaVA-OV Refusal Head training is augmented with 3,000 toxic images from ToViLaG with no test image overlap.
The inference pipeline extracts activations from the SAS-selected layer (Layer 20 for LLaVA-OV, Layer 24 for Chameleon) for each input.
The inference pipeline applies the safety prober to the extracted layer activation to produce a toxicity score, then conditionally activates the Refusal Head only when the score exceeds τ = 0.5.
The AutoSteer inference code uses ε = 0.1 for the steering intensity, matching the perturbation strength used during training.
The Steer (LM-Steer) baseline has been implemented with uniform, unconditional steering applied to all inputs regardless of toxicity.
The original model (Orig.) baseline evaluation code has been implemented for both LLaVA-OV and Chameleon without any steering applied.
The ASR evaluation code using GPT-4o as a binary classifier has been implemented with the exact prompt from Appendix B.2.
The general accuracy evaluation for LLaVA-OV has been implemented by direct comparison with ground-truth answers (single character, yes/no, or number).
The general accuracy evaluation for Chameleon has been implemented using regex matching to check if the ground-truth answer appears in the model output.
The code for training a separate prober at each layer and tracking prober accuracy across training epochs (for Figures 3, 9, 10) has been implemented.
The code for computing and comparing SAS scores across all evaluated layers for LLaVA-OV (layers 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24) and Chameleon (layers 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28) has been implemented.
The code for computing pairwise cosine similarity heatmaps of CAA vectors across layers (Figure 5, Figure 11-12) has been implemented.
The code for the ε sensitivity analysis, sweeping ε from 0 to beyond 0.2 and measuring ASR at each value, has been implemented for both LLaVA-OV and Chameleon.
The data construction pipeline (safe counterpart generation from VLSafe and ToViLaG+ augmentation) has been executed successfully without errors.
The SAS computation across all layers has been executed successfully for both LLaVA-OV and Chameleon, producing SAS scores per layer.
The safety prober training has been executed successfully for all evaluated layers of LLaVA-OV (layers 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24) and Chameleon (layers 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28).
The Refusal Head training has been executed successfully for both LLaVA-OV and Chameleon.
The main evaluation experiments (Table 1) have been executed successfully for LLaVA-OV on VLSafe Examine and all three ToViLaG+ test sets.
The main evaluation experiments (Table 1) have been executed successfully for Chameleon on VLSafe Examine and all three ToViLaG+ test sets.
The general capability evaluation on RealWorldQA (both models) and MMMU (LLaVA-OV) has been executed successfully without errors.
The ε sensitivity analysis experiment has been executed successfully for LLaVA-OV on VLSafe (Figure 6) and for Chameleon (Figure 8).
The prober accuracy analysis across training epochs for all layers has been executed successfully for both models (Figures 3, 9, 10).
The cosine similarity heatmap analysis of CAA vectors across layers has been executed successfully for both models (Figure 5).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: AutoSteer reduces LLaVA-OV ASR on VLSafe from 60.0% to 4.2%, while the Steer baseline achieves 2.0%.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: AutoSteer reduces LLaVA-OV ASR on ToViLaG+ text-only from 44.8% to 3.6%, and on image-only from 70.6% to 0.0%.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: AutoSteer fully preserves LLaVA-OV RealWorldQA accuracy at 61.8% (no degradation from original), outperforming Steer at 60.8%.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: AutoSteer preserves LLaVA-OV MMMU accuracy at 48.4%, matching the original and outperforming Steer at 47.8%.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: AutoSteer reduces Chameleon ASR on VLSafe from 67.8% to 15.4%, matching the Steer baseline.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: AutoSteer preserves Chameleon RealWorldQA accuracy at 6.0%, matching the original and outperforming Steer at 5.4%.
The execution outcomes match Figure 4: Layer 20 has the highest SAS score for LLaVA-OV (approximately 0.355) and Layer 24 has the highest for Chameleon (approximately 0.172).
The execution outcomes match Figure 3: most layers of LLaVA-OV achieve prober training accuracy above 98%, with early layers (4, 8) failing on image-only toxicity test sets.

The execution outcomes match Figure 5: later layers (e.g., Layer 20) of LLaVA-OV show stronger and more consistent cosine similarity alignment in safety-related CAA vectors compared to early layers (e.g., Layer 4).
The execution outcomes match Figure 6: LLaVA-OV ASR on VLSafe drops steeply as ε increases from 0 to approximately 0.05, then plateaus at low ASR for higher ε values.
The execution outcomes confirm that Chameleon has globally low SAS scores and no layer is effective on image-only toxicity subsets, consistent with its limited visual safety awareness.
The execution outcomes confirm that AutoSteer's selective/conditional steering avoids general capability degradation that is observed in the always-on Steer baseline.
